chapters.title: Beyond the comfort zone
chapters.text:
You interact with the world around you in two fundamental ways. Picture yourself in a cozy room. Looking down, you see familiar items — your smartphone, perhaps a cup of coffee, or a notebook. These things are within your reach, easy to interact with, and represent the moments you live in — the present. They anchor you, keep you grounded, and bring comfort.

However, when you look beyond your immediate surroundings, you spot the vast sky, soaring birds, or tall buildings. These aren't items you can grab. They stand for future aspirations, goals, and the unseen. To reach them, you must plan, dream, and act.

The main thing here is not what you see but what you feel. When you engage with the present, certain chemicals, the “here & nows,” flood your brain, usually a mix of neurotransmitters like serotonin, oxytocin, and endorphins. They help you revel in the moment, whether laughing at a joke, enjoying a meal, or hugging a loved one.

Yet, a different chemical — dopamine — kicks in when we set our sights higher, aspiring for more. You might have heard that dopamine is about immediate rewards, but it's also the anticipation of those rewards. When we think about our goals, dreams, or the prize of a future endeavor, dopamine is released. It pushes us beyond, making us chase, create, innovate, and explore the unknown.

Overcoming discomfort will bring undeniable benefits in the long run.

As you see, ambition and contentment are two sides of the same coin. Balance them, and you find harmony. Overdo one, and chaos can ensue. It's fascinating how these two perspectives — the contented present and the ambitious future — shape your life experiences. Their interplay defines your happiness, success, and even your challenges. But here's the most intriguing thing: the chemicals in your brain guide you.

Further, you can examine yourself from this point of view more carefully. It will be exciting, so stay tuned!

chapters.title: The restless mind molecule
chapters.text:
Have you ever wondered why you feel a constant urge to take control of situations or strive for more? The answer might lie in a single molecule in your brain: dopamine. Sure, it's responsible for that itch you feel to chase after something you want. But it's also the little voice helping you strategize and determine how to get there. However, like mindlessly following a GPS without checking the road ahead, being driven only by desire without a solid game plan can lead you to a dead end.

Check whether your short-term desires correspond with your values.

Interestingly, the dopamine-driven desire for control doesn't always appear as expected. Sometimes, getting the upper hand means taking a step back. Think about it this way: have you ever found yourself being extra nice to someone so that you can sway them to your side? That's your brain employing a 'domination through submission' strategy. Consider a fascinating example of this with stump-tailed macaque monkeys. When these critters get a dopamine boost, they don’t turn into little tyrants. Instead, they use gestures of submission, like lip-smacking, to get what they want.

Imagine you need a favor from a co-worker. Rather than demanding it, you might charm or befriend them, treating them with extra kindness or respect. By doing this, you're not bowing down to them but strategically working the room to get the outcome you desire.

Yet, there's another twist to the tale of dopamine that goes beyond cheering you on when you reach your goals. This driving force keeps you on your toes, constantly yearning for the next big thing. Regardless of where you are right now, whether you're content in a cozy apartment or living it up in a sprawling villa, that dopamine push will always have you eyeing the horizon for something even better.

Dopamine isn't the pleasure molecule, after all. It's the anticipation molecule. ~ Daniel Z. Lieberman, Michael E. Long

Daniel Lieberman,

All this means is that your brain, thanks to dopamine, is always plotting and planning. Whether strategizing the next big career move or figuring out how to achieve personal goals, dopamine is there, guiding, pushing, and sometimes pulling you in unexpected directions. Knowing this can offer a fresh perspective on why you do what you do, shedding light on the difference between desire and strategy in your mind.

chapters.title: The deceptive desire for novelty
chapters.text:
You know that feeling when you first fall in love? The excitement, the butterflies, and the sense of everything being fresh and new? Let's dive into why that feeling is both thrilling and, unfortunately, fleeting.

Consider Shawn's story. He seems to have everything going for him, yet he feels a deep sense of loneliness. He's looking for more than just physical closeness; he wants someone to share his life with and talk to at the end of the day. It's a feeling many of us can relate to, that longing for a meaningful connection.

Then there's Samantha. She's with Shawn but finds her thoughts drifting back to an old flame, Demarco. She's torn between her comfort and stability with Shawn and the exciting “what if” of rekindling an old romance with Demarco.

You might wonder why it is so hard to be content with what we have. Well, a big part of the answer lies in dopamine.

Dopamine is like your brain's “go-getter.” It doesn't kick in when you're relaxed and content but jumps into action when something new and exciting is on the horizon. It pushes you to explore, to seek out, and to desire. Think of it as the driving force that compels you to chase the unfamiliar and the unattained. It is why the early days of love feel so exhilarating. That new person in your life triggers a dopamine rush, making everything feel exciting and new.

But here's the kicker: that dopamine-driven excitement doesn't last forever. Over time, the novelty of a relationship wears off. The intense feelings at the start of a relationship — those stolen glances and heart-racing moments — start to wane. It's natural, and it's something all couples face. The challenge then becomes: how do we keep the love alive when the initial spark fades?

Make it a habit to compliment your partner daily to focus on their strengths.

For love to last, it needs to grow and evolve. That means moving beyond the initial dopamine-fueled excitement and building a deeper, more meaningful connection. Couples must work on finding joy in shared experiences, understanding each other's quirks, and building a life together. While the thrill of something new is enticing, there's profound beauty in the familiar. The valid reward lies in finding someone to run beside you for the long haul.

chapters.title: The personal among the imposed
chapters.text:
Have you ever been on a diet and suddenly found yourself completely drawn to the smell of freshly cooked burgers? You might think giving in to that craving will be the pinnacle of joy, but the pleasure isn't as intense as you expected as you take a bite. Why does that happen? Much of it boils down to — you guessed it — dopamine.

You see, dopamine is this fascinating little molecule that acts as the brain's internal “like” button, pushing us toward things we find rewarding. It paints a mental picture of what could be, urging us to make it a reality. But while dopamine is excellent at amping up the excitement of the pursuit, the joy you get when you finally have what you want often decreases over time.

Learn to notice joy and pleasure in the moment you experience them.

Consider Andrew's story. He consistently found himself drawn to attractive women. Every time he met someone new, it was like an adrenaline rush. But once the initial thrill faded, he felt a nagging sense of dissatisfaction. That's dopamine in action — it loves the chase more than the catch.

Now, think about how this plays out on a larger societal scale. Our collective behaviors, nudged by dopamine, set the trends and norms around us, like how everyone's rushing to buy the latest tech gadget or following the newest fashion trend. Before you know it, you're a part of this giant loop, driven by a desire for more. The clothes you wear, the gadgets you use, and even how you interact are all influenced by our collective desires.

But, and this is crucial, while dopamine can be your cheerleader, it can sometimes lead you down not-so-great paths. Have you ever found yourself binge-shopping online late at night? Or maybe having just one more slice of cake than you should? That's dopamine pushing you, even when it's not in your best interest. When the drive becomes too intense, some might find themselves in troubling situations, like drug abuse or other compulsive behaviors. It's like a double-edged sword — it can motivate but sometimes make you lose sight of what's best for you.

So, it's an invitation to pause, think, and maybe even recalibrate your pursuits, ensuring they bring genuine joy and not just a fleeting high.

chapters.title: Look for the golden mean
chapters.text:
William Plomer, a poet, once said that creativity is the power to connect the unconnected. It's like your brain suddenly lights up and links ideas you'd never put together. Creativity is a superpower, allowing you to develop innovative solutions and concepts that can change the world.

But there's a flip side to this coin. Imagine trying to navigate your day, and everything feels like a challenge. It's like the world around you is just too much to handle. That's a glimpse into mental illness. What's fascinating is that these two worlds — extreme creativity and mental challenges — are closely related. And guess what ties them together? Dopamine.

Now, here's something to chew on. Some people take medicines to increase their dopamine levels, especially those with Parkinson's disease. These meds can help manage their symptoms. But a surprising side effect? Some find their creative juices flowing like never before. Imagine being good at writing poems, and then after starting a new medication, you suddenly pen a poem that wins a major award. It sounds unreal, but it's true!

Let's dive a bit deeper. Have you ever had one of those flying or falling dreams? Or maybe you were chasing something just out of reach? These aren't random. Dreams, like our moments of creativity, are influenced by dopamine. They take real-life experiences but mix them up in unexpected, sometimes bizarre ways. It's like your brain is playing a familiar yet entirely new movie.

However, as much as you might want to always ride the high of creativity, it's important to remember that balance is vital. As incredible as your brain is, it is also fragile. If unchecked, the same rush of inspiration that leads to a game-changing idea can also tip you into a whirlwind of confusion. It's like walking a tightrope: on one side, you have this incredible ability to think outside the box, and on the other, you could lose touch with reality.

Alternate periods of creative binging with grounding.

So, the next time you're brainstorming, take a moment to appreciate the wonders of your mind.

Did you know? The Ancient Greeks saw creativity as a divine gift from the Muses, Zeus's nine artistic daughters.

chapters.title: Consumerism with responsibility
chapters.text:
Imagine, for a moment, that you're traveling back in time to Africa around 200,000 years ago. It's here that your ancient ancestors began an extraordinary journey. Around 100,000 years after their evolution, they felt a compelling urge to leave their homeland. Why? Not just out of sheer curiosity but out of pure necessity. By staying in one place, they'd be easy targets for threats like diseases, droughts, or any unforeseen disaster that might come their way.

So, off they went, traveling to different parts of the world. Now, here's where things get super interesting. When you look at our DNA today, it's pretty much the same across the board. It hints at a time in our history when shockingly few humans were around. At one point, our numbers dropped to less than 20,000. This close call shows how important it was for our ancestors to spread out. Living in different places ensured a disaster in one spot wouldn't wipe out all humanity. Branching out is life's backup plan.

At the same time, dopamine kept nudging them to always want more. More land, more resources, more everything.

On the plus side, this “wanting more” trait is why you love innovations and consistently seek to grow and improve. It's why humans, as a species, have made incredible progress. But, like too much of a good thing, this drive can backfire. For example, our ancestors' movement to new places was great for survival but not so great for the environment and other species living there. Humans took over, used up resources, and sometimes pushed other species out of the way.

Fast forward to today. People want more, achieve more, and build more, but at what cost? The planet's resources are not infinite. And if this dopamine-driven desire for “more” goes unchecked, it could change our world in ways we might not even recognize.

Always check whether you harm someone or something to achieve your desires.

The same instinct that once kept us alive could also be our Achilles' heel. The challenge for all of us is to find a balance, to harness that drive for the greater good, and ensure a brighter, sustainable future.

From dopamine's point of view, having things is uninteresting. It's only getting things that matters. ~ Daniel Z. Lieberman, Michael E. Long

Daniel Lieberman,

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
On one side, you have dopamine, which motivates us to chase our dreams, set goals, and always look ahead. Conversely, you have the “here & now” neurotransmitters that remind you to stay present and appreciate your current situation.

And here's the catch: if you are always looking forward, you might miss out on the good things happening right now. Imagine being so focused on tomorrow that even minor problems today feel like massive disasters. It can occur if there's too much emphasis on the future and not enough on the present. Yet, if you are always in the present, you might not make plans or work toward better things in the future. So, you need a mix of both. With a bit of tweaking in how your brain works, it's possible to find happiness and cope better with challenges.

Harmony is essentially about striking a balance. Thus, your goal is to blend your ambitions with an awareness of today. It means that while you might be excited about the weekend, you should also enjoy your weekday tasks. Or, while looking forward to a special event, you can still find happiness in the smaller moments leading up to it.

In simple terms, harmony means mixing your plans with your current joys. It's a way to fully experience life, appreciate what you have, and look forward to what's coming next.

Try this


• Make a clear to-do list for the day, but also set aside “me-time.”
• Write down three things you're grateful for before bed.
• Choose an activity you love and do it for fun, not because you must.
• Spend quality time with someone you care about daily, even if it’s just a short chat.

Read it till the end?
